original_abstract:
While recent advances in neural forecasting have improved the performance of large-scale systems, long-horizon prediction remains a significant challenge. Two major hurdles in this area are the high volatility of forecasts and the prohibitive computational complexity of existing models. To address these issues, we introduce NHITS, a model that integrates two novel techniques: multi-rate data sampling and hierarchical interpolation.

These methods allow the model to assemble predictions sequentially, decomposing the input signal to emphasize components across a variety of frequencies and scales. Mathematically, we prove that hierarchical interpolation can efficiently approximate arbitrarily long forecasting horizons when the underlying signal is smooth. 

We validated our approach through extensive experiments on large-scale benchmark datasets. NHITS consistently outperforms current state-of-the-art methods, achieving an average accuracy improvement of nearly 20% over the latest Transformer-based architectures. Remarkably, it does so while reducing computational time by an order of magnitude—completing tasks up to 50 times faster than its competitors. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Nixtla/neuralforecast.
humanized_abstract:
Although neural forecasting has advanced in the last few years and many large-scale systems have been forecast with great accuracy, long-horizon forecasting is still a challenge. The volatility of forecasts is very high and the computational cost is high as well. In this paper, we propose a new model called NHITS (Neural Hierarchical Interpolation with Sampling) that combines two innovations: multi-rate data sampling and hierarchical interpolation.

These methods enable the model to generate predictions in a sequential fashion, dissecting the input signal and focusing on different aspects at various frequencies and scales. We show via mathematical proofs that hierarchical interpolation can achieve accurate long term forecasts given that the signal is sufficiently smooth.

We validate the effectiveness of our approach on a wide range of large-scale benchmark datasets. NHITS delivers superior performance to state-of-the-art models, with an average improvement of nearly 20% when compared to the latest variants of Transformer based models. At the same time, it achieves faster inference time up to 10x (i.e., up to 50x faster than the baseline models). Code for our approach is available at https://github.com/Nixtla/neuralforecast.

We show via mathematical proofs that hierarchical interpolation can achieve accurate long term forecasts given that the signal is sufficiently smooth.

We validate the effectiveness of our approach on a wide range of large-scale benchmark datasets. document.input:
While recent advances in neural forecasting have improved the performance of large-scale systems, long-horizon prediction remains a significant challenge. Two major hurdles in this area are the high volatility of forecasts and the prohibitive computational complexity of existing models. To address these issues, we introduce NHITS, a model that integrates two novel techniques: multi-rate data sampling and hierarchical interpolation.

These methods allow the model to assemble predictions sequentially, decomposing the input signal to emphasize components across a variety of frequencies and scales. Mathematically, we prove that hierarchical interpolation can efficiently approximate arbitrarily long forecasting horizons when the underlying signal is smooth. 

We validated our approach through extensive experiments on large-scale benchmark datasets. NHITS consistently outperforms current state-of-the-art methods, achieving an average accuracy improvement of nearly 20% over the latest Transformer-based architectures. Remarkably, it does so while reducing computational time by an order of magnitude—completing tasks up to 50 times faster than its competitors. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Nixtla/neuralforecast.
